facts.fact: Cold exposure at 50-60°F (10-15°C) for 1-3 minutes increases dopamine levels by up to 250% and norepinephrine by 530%, with effects lasting hours
Cold exposure increases adrenaline and dopamine via activation of brown adipose tissue (BAT) and cold shock proteins
Recommended cold plunge protocol: 3-4 sessions per week, 1-3 minutes at 50-60°F (10-15°C), progress to colder/longer gradually
For fat loss via cold exposure: aim for 8-11 minutes total per week in cold water, spread across 2-4 sessions
Sleep toolkit: View sunlight within 30-60 minutes of waking to set circadian rhythm; delay caffeine 90-120 minutes after waking
Optimal sleep environment: room temperature 60-67°F (15-19°C), pitch dark, no screens 30-60 min before bed
Magnesium threonate or glycinate (200-400mg) before bed can improve sleep onset and depth
L-theanine (100-200mg) before bed reduces sleep latency and improves sleep quality
Apigenin (50mg) before bed acts as a mild sedative via GABA receptors, improving sleep onset
Inositol (900mg) before bed can help reduce nighttime awakenings and anxiety-driven sleep disruption
Glycine (3g) before bed can lower core body temperature and improve sleep quality
NSDR (Non-Sleep Deep Rest) / yoga nidra for 10-30 min during the day can offset sleep debt and reduce stress
Morning sunlight (5-10 min outdoors, not through windows) triggers cortisol spike and dopamine release that sets circadian rhythm for the entire day
Physiological sigh: double inhale through nose followed by long exhale through mouth; most effective rapid stress reduction technique (1-3 breaths)
Cold water immersion at 15°C for 1 hour increased norepinephrine 3x and dopamine 2.5x in research studies
Brown adipose tissue (BAT) activation through cold exposure can increase resting metabolic rate by up to 15%
Cold exposure post-exercise may blunt hypertrophy adaptations; avoid within 4-6 hours after resistance training if muscle growth is the goal
Cold plunge temperature progression: beginners start at 60°F (15°C) for 1 min, advanced can go to 45°F (7°C) for 3-5 min
Cold exposure activates cold shock proteins (CIRP, RBM3) that have neuroprotective effects and may improve cognitive function

sources.source_name: PMC - Cold Exposure & Brown Adipose Tissue Meta-Analysis

facts.fact: Acute cold exposure significantly increases energy expenditure via brown adipose tissue activation; BAT can contribute 5-30% of resting metabolic rate during cold exposure
Repeated cold exposure leads to BAT recruitment and increased thermogenic capacity, improving metabolic flexibility over time
Cold-induced thermogenesis is impaired in individuals with higher BMI/obesity, suggesting BAT dysfunction in metabolic disease

sources.source_name: Discovery Journals - Finnish Sauna Bathing in Cardiovascular Health

facts.fact: Regular Finnish sauna bathing (4-7 sessions/week at 80-100°C) is associated with 50% reduction in cardiovascular disease mortality vs. 1 session/week
Sauna bathing acutely lowers blood pressure, improves endothelial function, and reduces arterial stiffness
Sauna use mimics cardiovascular exercise: heart rate rises to 120-150 bpm, cardiac output increases 60-70%, blood flow redirects to skin
Heat shock proteins (HSPs) induced by sauna protect against protein misfolding, reduce inflammation, and improve insulin sensitivity
Sauna use is associated with reduced risk of Alzheimer's and dementia - up to 65% lower risk with 4-7 sessions/week
Recommended sauna protocol: 80-100°C, 15-30 minutes per session, 4-7 times per week for optimal cardiovascular and longevity benefits
Sauna is safe for most individuals including those with stable cardiovascular disease; caution advised for unstable angina or recent MI
Zone 2 training: exercise at 55-75% of max heart rate (or RPE 5-6/10) where you can hold a conversation but it's slightly strained; 180+ minutes/week minimum
Zone 2 training improves mitochondrial function and metabolic flexibility — the ability to use fat as primary fuel source at higher intensities
Poor metabolic fitness (assessed via VO2 max) is the single strongest predictor of all-cause mortality — stronger than smoking, diabetes, or heart disease
Exercise is the single most potent longevity drug: includes strength training (3-4x/week), Zone 2 cardio (3+ hours/week), VO2 max intervals (1x/week), and stability work
Peter Attia's exercise framework: 4 pillars - strength, stability, Zone 2 aerobic, VO2 max/anaerobic. Each week should include all 4
For someone at 130kg, weight loss is the #1 longevity intervention — even 10% body weight reduction dramatically improves insulin sensitivity, inflammation, and cardiovascular markers
Ashwagandha (250-600mg/day for 8 weeks) significantly reduces perceived stress and cortisol levels compared to placebo
Ashwagandha (600mg/day with resistance training for 8 weeks) produced significantly greater gains in muscle strength and size vs placebo
Ashwagandha significantly enhances VO2 max in healthy adults and athletes (analysis of 5 studies)
Ashwagandha increased testosterone by 14.7% and DHEA-S by 18% in overweight males aged 40-70 taking extract daily for 8 weeks
Ashwagandha significantly increased sperm concentration, semen volume, and sperm motility in males with low sperm count (review of 4 studies)
Ashwagandha reduces blood sugar, HbA1c, insulin, and oxidative stress markers (review of 24 studies including 5 clinical studies in diabetics)
Ashwagandha (600mg/day for 12 weeks) significantly improved sleep quality and mental alertness upon waking in adults 65-80
Ashwagandha improved immediate and general memory, attention, and information-processing speed in adults taking 600mg/day for 8 weeks
Creatine loading phase: 20g/day (4x5g) for 5-7 days, then maintenance dose of 3-5g/day
95% of body's creatine stores are in muscles (as phosphocreatine); 5% in brain and testes
Creatine increases ATP production capacity, enabling more total work/volume in training sessions — key for long-term muscle growth
Creatine reduces myostatin levels, increasing muscle growth potential
Creatine supplementation improves short-term memory and intelligence, with strongest effects in older adults and vegetarians
Creatine is the single most effective supplement for adding muscle mass (large review of most popular supplements)
Creatine monohydrate is the best-researched, most effective, and cheapest form; other forms lack evidence of superiority
Studies up to 4 years show no negative effects of creatine supplementation at standard doses in healthy individuals
Creatine may lower blood sugar levels and improve glucose metabolism
Without loading phase, taking 3-5g/day takes ~4 weeks to maximize muscle creatine stores
Vitamin D regulates calcium/phosphorus absorption and facilitates healthy immune system function
Low vitamin D levels linked to increased risk of multiple sclerosis, heart disease, and severe respiratory infections
Vitamin D supplementation improved depression symptoms in people with deficiency (review of 7,534 people)
People with higher body weights have greater chance of low vitamin D levels — vitamin D is fat-soluble and sequestered in adipose tissue
RDA for adults 18-70: 600 IU (15 mcg); adults over 70: 800 IU (20 mcg); many experts recommend 1000-4000 IU for optimal levels
Optimal blood serum levels: 50-100 nmol/L; deficiency is common in obese individuals and those with limited sun exposure
Wim Hof Method (breathing + cold exposure + commitment) has been shown in studies to voluntarily influence the autonomic nervous system and immune response
WIM Hof breathing involves 30-40 deep breaths followed by a breath hold (retention) — triggers brief respiratory alkalosis and mild hypoxia
Practitioners of Wim Hof Method showed reduced inflammatory markers (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha) when injected with endotoxin, vs. control group who had strong inflammatory response
Wim Hof Method increased epinephrine (adrenaline) levels more than 3x baseline during breathing exercises
WHM has been shown to improve glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity in healthy volunteers after 10 days of practice
Red light therapy (RLT) uses low-wavelength red light (630-660nm) and near-infrared light (810-850nm) to stimulate cellular function
RLT may improve skin health by stimulating collagen production, reducing wrinkles, and improving skin tone
RLT may reduce inflammation and pain, with studies showing benefits for osteoarthritis, tendonitis, and muscle recovery
RLT works via photobiomodulation — light photons absorbed by mitochondria (cytochrome c oxidase) increase ATP production and reduce oxidative stress
Red light therapy is generally considered safe with few side effects; main risk is eye damage if used without protection
Many RLT claims (hair growth, weight loss, cancer treatment) lack strong evidence — marketing often outpaces science
Body fat reduction is the most effective natural testosterone optimizer — fat tissue converts testosterone to estrogen via aromatase enzyme
Compound lifts (squat, deadlift, bench press) acutely boost testosterone more than isolation exercises; heavy resistance training (3-5 reps, 85-95% 1RM) is optimal
Chronic calorie deficit and overtraining suppress testosterone; adequate calories and recovery are essential for hormone optimization
Vitamin D (2000-4000 IU/day), zinc (15-30mg/day), and magnesium (200-400mg/day) are the most evidence-based micronutrients for testosterone support
Sleep deprivation (less than 6 hours/night) can reduce testosterone by 10-15% within one week
Cortisol and testosterone are inversely related — chronic stress management is critical for maintaining healthy testosterone levels
Bryan Johnson's Blueprint involves 100+ daily supplements, strict vegan diet (~2,250 calories), and extensive biomarker tracking — extreme protocol, not evidence-based for general population
Blueprint protocol includes NMN (NR precursor for NAD+) 1000mg/day, metformin for anti-aging (off-label), and rapamycin (experimental, prescription-only)
Johnson's measurable results: slowed epigenetic aging by 5.1 years (per DunedinPACE), reduced inflammation markers to top 0.1% of 20-year-olds
Blueprint separates evidence-based practices (sleep, exercise, diet) from experimental (NMN, rapamycin, plasma exchange) — the former are universally recommended, the latter are N=1 experiments
Cold exposure at 14°C for 1 hour increased metabolic rate by 80% in cold-adapted individuals with active brown fat
Cold showers (20°C, 2-3 min) are a gentler alternative to ice baths; still provide dopamine and norepinephrine boost but less BAT activation
Contrast therapy (alternating hot/cold) may be superior for DOMS recovery than cold alone, by creating vascular pump effect

sources.source_name: General Biohacking Knowledge (synthesized from multiple sources)

facts.fact: Box breathing (4-4-4-4: inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s) activates parasympathetic nervous system, reducing heart rate and stress within 2-5 minutes
Buteyko breathing method focuses on nasal breathing and CO2 tolerance; may help asthma and sleep apnea but lacks robust RCT evidence
Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA, 1-3g/day combined) reduce inflammation, improve cardiovascular health, and support brain function. Strong evidence for triglyceride reduction
NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide) and NR (Nicotinamide Riboside) are NAD+ precursors; animal studies show longevity benefits but human evidence is early-stage
NMN human trials: 250mg/day for 12 weeks improved insulin sensitivity and aerobic capacity in prediabetic adults (2023 Sinclair lab study)
Metformin (off-label anti-aging): TAME trial ongoing; observational data suggests diabetics on metformin have lower cancer and all-cause mortality than non-diabetics
Rapamycin: mTOR inhibitor that extends lifespan in mice by 20-25%; human anti-aging use is experimental and prescription-only. Weekly dosing (3-6mg) is most common off-label protocol

Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGMs) like Libre/Dexcom show real-time glucose response to food, stress, sleep, and exercise. For non-diabetics: optimal fasting glucose 70-90 mg/dL, post-meal spikes <140 mg/dL, return to baseline <2 hours

Time-restricted eating (TRE) / intermittent fasting: 12-14 hour daily eating window minimum for metabolic benefits; 16:8 is most studied protocol. Benefits include improved insulin sensitivity, autophagy, and weight management
Mouth taping for sleep: promotes nasal breathing, reduces snoring and sleep apnea severity; limited RCTs but growing clinical interest. Use porous medical tape, not duct/strong tape
Oura Ring tracks sleep stages, HRV, body temperature, and activity; Whoop band tracks recovery, strain, and sleep. Both help quantify sleep quality and recovery trends
Blue light blocking glasses: amber-tinted glasses 2-3 hours before bed improve melatonin production and sleep onset. Evidence strongest for LED/screen-heavy environments

Compression boots (Normatec): peristaltic compression improves circulation and lymphatic drainage; reduces DOMS and perceived fatigue. Evidence is moderate — not dramatically better than active recovery
Massage guns (percussive therapy): reduce muscle soreness and improve short-term range of motion; equivalent to manual massage for DOMS relief. 30-60 seconds per muscle group post-workout
EMS/TENS devices: electrical muscle stimulation can supplement rehabilitation and maintain muscle in immobilized limbs; limited value for healthy athletes beyond recovery

PEMF (Pulsed Electromagnetic Field) therapy: FDA-approved for bone healing and post-surgical recovery; evidence for general recovery/wellness is limited. Mostly marketing hype for consumer wellness devices

Infrared saunas operate at lower temperatures (50-60°C) than traditional Finnish saunas (80-100°C); fewer cardiovascular studies but may offer similar heat shock protein benefits with less heat stress
Growth hormone release: 2x 20-minute sauna sessions at 80°C separated by 30-min cooling can increase GH by 2-5x; effect is amplified by exercise beforehand

Cortisol management: chronic elevation (from stress, poor sleep, overtraining) suppresses testosterone, immune function, and promotes abdominal fat storage. Key interventions: sleep, meditation, ashwagandha, reducing caffeine
Thyroid health: iodine, selenium, and zinc are critical cofactors for thyroid hormone production. Hypothyroidism can cause weight gain, fatigue, and cold intolerance

Metabolic flexibility: ability to efficiently switch between glucose and fat oxidation. Improved by Zone 2 training, intermittent fasting, and low-carb periods. Key marker: low fasting insulin (<5 mIU/L)
Rhodiola rosea (200-400mg/day, standardized to 3% rosavins): adaptogen that reduces mental fatigue and improves stress resilience. Evidence moderate for chronic fatigue; weak for athletic performance
L-theanine (100-200mg): amino acid from green tea; promotes alpha brain waves (relaxed alertness). Synergistic with caffeine for focus without jitters
Magnesium glycinate (200-400mg): highly bioavailable form; supports sleep, muscle recovery, and 300+ enzymatic reactions. Most adults are deficient
Magnesium threonate: only form that crosses blood-brain barrier; supports cognitive function and neuroplasticity. More expensive, used for brain health specifically

David Sinclair protocol: NMN 1000mg/day, resveratrol, metformin 850mg twice daily (off-label). Based on animal aging research; human evidence still emerging. Sinclair personally takes these but acknowledges N=1 nature
Peter Attia's personal protocol emphasizes: 4 hours exercise/week minimum (strength + Zone 2 + VO2 max), 7.5+ hours sleep, protein at 1g/lb lean mass, minimal alcohol, strong social connections

For obese individuals (130kg), priority hierarchy for health optimization: (1) weight loss via caloric deficit + protein, (2) strength training to preserve/build muscle during weight loss, (3) Zone 2 cardio for metabolic health, (4) sleep optimization, (5) cold/heat/breathwork/supplements as adjuncts
Protein intake for 130kg male: 1.6-2.2g protein per kg of target body weight (not current weight). If target is 85kg: 136-187g protein/day
NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis): walking 8,000-12,000 steps/day can burn 300-500 extra calories — critical for weight loss without overtraining
